claims.conclusion: A narrower Rust-only implementation is proven, but the claim is materially overbroad and assigns ownership imprecisely. At nu_plugin commit 9451b20e42d79f72d4fa5b2a190a39bd1d9d5e5a, Nushell is the plugin host/table cockpit; Rust code recursively enumerates the selected root, selects files classified as Rust source, reads them as UTF-8, parses them with syn, and emits syntax-only rows including functions and structs. Cargo dependency rows are obtained separately by invoking `cargo metadata --format-version 1`, not mapped from the Rust AST. The active profile-owned CodeDB 0.1.0 binary reproduced the function/struct rows and captured real symlink metadata, but it also traversed `.git` (437 rows) and `target` (13,738 rows), so ignored/generated boundaries are classified rather than excluded. Polyglot AST support remains documented as candidates/planning, not current implementation. The complete required fixture corpus was not executable under the strict no-write constraint because there is no committed malformed-Rust fixture and the dependency fixture has no Cargo.lock while the product's metadata command lacks `--locked`; therefore the exact broad claim is qualified, not verified.

evidence.detail: The canonical extract says source wording proves no implementation and records INGEST-CLAIM-001 only as an asserted current-implementation claim.
The canonical row marks the claim source-only/unverified and requires a pinned parser trace plus fixtures for languages, symlinks, ignored paths, malformed files, and dependencies.
The product contract identifies Nushell as table cockpit/plugin host and the Rust extractor as the owner of compiler/crate fact capture.
The Rust Nushell plugin registers scan, Cargo dependency, and Rust item commands.
`scan_summary_rows` separately invokes filesystem scan, Rust item extraction, and Cargo metadata capture; its dependency summary explicitly describes Cargo metadata rows.
`rust_item_rows` iterates Rust source paths and calls `capture_rust_items`; parse errors are propagated rather than converted to validation rows.
The plugin derives parser inputs only from filesystem entries classified as `RustSource`.
The pinned Nushell command entry point is `codedb scan`; its Rust `run` method resolves the positional repository path and calls `scan_summary_rows`.

`scan_filesystem` recursively reads every directory, sorts children and output deterministically, records symlink targets, and does not follow symlink directories. There is no ignore/exclusion gate in this recursion.
Only `.rs` is classified as Rust source. Hidden/vendor/generated paths are classified after traversal; they are not excluded.
The Rust static extractor depends on syn major version 2 with full/parsing features; Cargo.lock pins syn 2.0.118.
`capture_rust_items` reads a Rust source file as text, calls `syn::parse_file`, recursively collects syntax items, deterministically sorts rows, and returns parse/read errors.
The AST collector emits module, function, struct, enum, trait, alias, const, static, impl, and use rows. It does not derive Cargo dependency edges.
Dependency capture shells out to `cargo metadata --format-version 1 --manifest-path ...` and parses JSON; it does not pass `--locked` or `--no-deps`.
Cargo package dependency objects, not AST nodes, are converted into `CargoDependencyRow` records.
The committed Rust unit fixture checks deterministic syntax-only rows including `Thing` as a struct and `make` as a function. This test was inspected but not rerun because it creates temporary files.

The Unix test specifies that scanner output records a symlink and target without following it. Current installed runtime output independently recorded three real result symlinks and their Nix-store targets.
A committed unit test expects a path dependency row and deterministic metadata output, but it creates a temporary workspace and was not rerun in this no-write pass.
The current global `validation_errors` implementation always returns an empty vector; malformed Rust instead aborts through the propagated parser error path.
Rust is the only lane described as existing; Python, Ruby, JS/TS, Go, and other language parsers are listed as candidates or later work.
Tree-sitter, ast-grep, Oxc, SWC, Prism, LibCST, Ruff, and go/parser are candidate/research/gated tools, not current parser implementations.
The committed fixture matrix covers Rust/Cargo, assets, and a symlink manifest family, but contains no malformed-Rust or ignored-path fixture and no implemented polyglot AST corpus.

missing_evidence: A committed malformed-Rust fixture and a read-only runtime receipt showing the exact error/validation contract. Current code propagates a parser error and leaves `validation_errors` empty.

A current execution receipt for `fixtures/workspace_two_crates` proving dependency rows/edges. It was not run because `capture_cargo_metadata` invokes cargo metadata without `--locked`, the fixture has no Cargo.lock, and this pass forbids generated files or repository mutation.
Implemented and executed parser fixtures for languages beyond Rust. Current polyglot documents list parser candidates/plans only.

A Nushell-session receipt through the active profile `yzx` proving `nu_plugin_codedb` registration and command execution. The profile plugin binary and installed CodeDB CLI are present, but no direct profile `nu` symlink or generated runtime plugin reference was available without launching/materializing runtime.

A provenance receipt binding the installed Nix-store CodeDB binary exactly to git commit 9451b20; binary identity, version, hash, and matching outputs were proven, but source revision metadata was not embedded in the observed output.

claims.conclusion: Current primary evidence contradicts the exact implementation claim. The current nu_plugin_codedb surface declares Type::Nothing input, every inspected command run method ignores `_input`, and the plugin crate has no codedb-store-redb/redb dependency; it produces native Nushell table values from repository/path arguments instead of receiving structured blocks. The pinned redb 4.1.0 writer exists in a separate codedb CLI path and persists `(relative_path, bytes)` batches scanned from the filesystem. Existing redb tests cover durable byte batches, checkpoint resume, future-schema refusal, and writer contention, but there is no plugin-output-to-redb call path or structured-block schema to replay. Installed profile ownership also keeps separate `codedb` and `nu_plugin_codedb` binaries; binary probes found redb writer symbols only in `codedb`, while the plugin contains the explicit text `redb store is not implemented until CDB015`. No write-producing fixture/test command was run because this pass was strictly read-only.

evidence.detail: The canonical extract says source wording proves no implementation and records INGEST-CLAIM-002 only as the source assertion.
The claim row itself says the named plugin, record schema, write path, and durability contract are absent and requires ordered, duplicate, malformed, interrupted, and concurrent replay.
The plugin depends on parser/core/Nushell/serialization crates only; it does not depend on codedb-store-redb or redb.
Plugin command signatures declare Type::Nothing input and table output; `--store` is only a filepath flag on the signature.
Macro-generated plugin commands bind `_input: &Value` but never read it; they compute rows and return Nushell values.
The concrete scan command also ignores `_input`, scans a repository path, and returns structured rows without opening a store.

The backend-neutral writer contract accepts batches of `(relative_path, bytes)`, promises input-order result rows and SHA-256 content deduplication, and exposes a resume skip-set; it is not a structured-block contract.
The separate codedb-store-redb crate owns the redb dependency.
The lockfile pins redb 4.1.0 with checksum 8e925444704b5f17d32bf42f5b6e2df050bceebc3dcd6e71cc73dafe8092e839.
CaptureBatcher opens redb and persist_batch_db writes content-addressed raw byte blobs, path rows, and metadata in one write transaction, then commits.
The separate codedb CLI chooses the local `.codedb/store.redb` backend, opens it, obtains resume paths, and calls `store.persist_batch`.

The CLI iterates filesystem entries, reads file bytes, batches `(relative_path, bytes)`, records unsupported entries as gaps, and checkpoints the final batch; plugin-produced blocks do not enter this path.
Tests exercise durable byte batches and drop/reopen checkpoint resume, not plugin structured blocks.
Tests reject an unknown schema and verify single-writer contention/release; they do not establish plugin-to-writer linkage.
The Nix package builds `codedb` and `nu_plugin_codedb` as separate binaries.
The package uses Cargo.lock and installs each binary separately with explicit runtime metadata.

The active Yazelix profile resolves both CodeDB commands to this bundled 0.1.0 package. A read-only strings probe found the redb writer symbols in `codedb`, not in `nu_plugin_codedb`; the plugin contained `redb store is not implemented until CDB015`.

missing_evidence: No plugin-to-redb call path exists in current source or the installed binary, so there is no structured-block schema/version receipt or plugin record hash to validate.

No ordered, duplicate, or malformed structured-block fixtures exist. The store contract discusses input order and content-addressed byte deduplication, but current tests do not replay these as plugin blocks.
The interrupted test is a committed-batch drop/reopen simulation, not process-kill, torn-write, or fault-injection crash evidence.
The concurrency test covers redb single-writer blocking/release, not concurrent plugin block producers.
Strict read-only scope prohibited running cargo/Nushell fixture tests because they create target/temp/store/generated files. Existing source tests were inspected but not replayed.

Installed profile paths, package metadata, version, and binary symbol separation were proven, but an end-to-end Nushell plugin protocol run was not performed because plugin registration/fixture replay would require writable config or temporary generated state.

claims.conclusion: Current primary evidence contradicts the asserted envctl pipeline. Envctl's contract forbids reading CodeDB redb internals; its default-off semantic module reads exported JSONL rows that already contain embedding_minilm vectors and builds only an in-memory HNSW index. No tracked caller of SemanticIndex was found. Envctl's ruvector-postgres dependency is an optional, default-off server-side extension, explicitly not a PostgreSQL client. A separate CodeDB implementation in the nu_plugin repository can capture raw file bytes directly to either redb or PostgreSQL through alternative BlobStore backends, but its PostgreSQL table explicitly omits the optional embedding column and its parity test independently writes the same fixture to both stores rather than transferring redb blocks to PostgreSQL. No envctl redb-to-PostgreSQL call path, embedding-generation owner/model, transaction receipt, or repeat/recovery run exists in the inspected evidence. Installed behavior was not proven: envctl is absent from PATH and from /home/flexnetos/.nix-profile/bin; only a source-build symlink exists under /home/flexnetos/meta/usr/bin, which is not an active profile frontdoor.

evidence.detail: The canonical row itself labels the assertion source-only and unverified and says no envctl call path, embedding owner/model, PostgreSQL receipt, or commit semantics are supplied.
INGEST-CLAIM-003 remains queued and requires pinned envctl/embedding inputs, a selected-record manifest, PostgreSQL transaction/row receipt, vector dimension/hash receipt, and recovery proof.

POSTGRES-004/005/006/008/009 are Draft definition/proof tasks; their excluded scopes include live ingestion, live embeddings, live redb/PostgreSQL mutation, and product implementation. POSTGRES-009 describes the desired bounded end-to-end proof, not a completed implementation.
The only envctl semantic module is a default-OFF ruvector consumer over exported CodeDB rows; the rows are documented as already carrying embedding_minilm vectors.
ExportedRow accepts an optional Vec<f32> embedding_minilm field. This is an input vector, not an embedding-generation configuration or model invocation.
from_export_jsonl parses exported lines, skips rows without vectors, and forwards existing vectors to from_rows. It neither reads redb nor generates embeddings nor writes PostgreSQL.
Tests cover in-memory top-k and dimension mismatch handling using literal vectors. They do not test model pinning, redb selection, PostgreSQL commits, idempotent replay, or crash recovery.
ruvector-postgres is explicitly described as a separate default-OFF server-side pgrx extension, not a client library.
Default features are empty; ruvector-postgres is optional and enabled only by ruvector-pg. No PostgreSQL client dependency or ingest transaction implementation is declared here.
CodeDB owns blob/code facts; envctl is a downstream export/checksum consumer and must not read redb internals or derive Rust/crate facts itself.
Every runtime row requires redb_access=forbidden. Redb restores blobs by SHA-256 before envctl consumes exported materialization rows.
This is CodeDB, not envctl. It implements a PostgreSQL BlobStore and persists one upsert per file in a single transaction.
The PostgreSQL backend creates a raw file/blob table explicitly minus the optional embedding column; there is no vector field or embedding receipt.

persist_batch hashes and upserts raw file bytes, then commits the transaction. It does not read redb or generate/store embeddings, and it returns source-file rows rather than a durable transaction receipt.
CodeDB selects PostgreSQL or redb as alternative capture/read backends based on --store; there is no redb-to-PostgreSQL transfer path.
Capture scans the source repository directly and opens one selected backend. The resume checkpoint is read from that same backend, not transferred from redb to PostgreSQL.
evidence.relationship: capture source identity and resume behavior

evidence.detail: The ignored live test independently persists one fixture batch to a fresh redb store and a temporary PostgreSQL table, then compares snapshots. It is backend parity, not an envctl redb-to-PostgreSQL pipeline and has no embedding stage.
The only live PostgreSQL/redb test is ignored and opt-in via CODEDB_PG_TEST=1; it was not run because this verification is read-only and forbids database mutation.

missing_evidence: A profile-owned active envctl binary/frontdoor and proof that the inspected source commit is what the active runtime executes.
A callable envctl entrypoint that selects a bounded redb source and transfers its records to PostgreSQL; current contracts forbid direct redb access and current source exposes no such path.
Pinned embedding configuration including model owner/name/version or artifact digest, tokenizer/config digest, vector dimension, normalization, and deterministic input hash.
A selected-source manifest identifying the redb database, schema/version, table or block IDs, source repository commit/root hash, and exact bounded record set.

A PostgreSQL transaction receipt containing transaction or run identity, target database/schema/table, committed row count, per-row/source hashes, vector hashes/dimensions, and commit/rollback outcome.
Repeat/idempotency evidence for the exact same source and embedding configuration, plus interrupted-run/crash recovery evidence and proof against duplicate or partial rows.

A live read-only-safe receipt of the existing ignored CodeDB PG/redb parity test; the test itself mutates a temporary PostgreSQL table and redb tempfile and therefore was not run in this pass. Even if run, it would prove backend blob parity, not the asserted envctl embedding pipeline.

claims.conclusion: The core statement is proven only as captured source framing: NBSOURCE-030 presents parser, redb buffer, and redb-to-PostgreSQL bridge atoms and summarizes them as a Phase 1 three-step flat-file-to-database proposal. It is not yet a committed, accepted, or implemented pipeline. Reconciliation against the committed graph is: (1) flat files -> AST blocks: FOUNDATION-001 must pin the sole parser/runtime package owner and POSTGRES-004 must define a read-only, bounded collector with stable source identity, byte/metadata hashes, AST schema, provenance, ordering, checkpoints, failure recovery, and lossless reconstruction; gate on a pinned parser plus deterministic fixture corpus and failure receipts. (2) AST blocks -> redb staging: require a versioned block schema, exact record hashes, ordered/idempotent writes, deduplication, crash replay, recovery source, and an explicit transient-versus-durable authority decision; gate on ordered/duplicate/malformed/interrupted/concurrent fixtures and STORE-001/PGAUTH-002 owner approval. This stage is not adequately represented by the claim's four aggregate refs and instead appears in adjacent draft tasks PGAUTH-002, POSTGRES-006, STORE-001, and the INGEST-CLAIM-002 queue row. (3) redb -> embedded PostgreSQL: require selected-record identity, pinned embedding model/configuration, transactional row/vector receipts, provenance, repeat/recovery behavior, and one-way authority into PostgreSQL; gate on a bounded transaction test, vector dimension/hash receipts, and deterministic round-trip projection. POSTGRES-008 is specifically the reverse database-to-directory projection contract, not the forward ingestion bridge, so POSTGRES-005/006/009 and PGAUTH-004 are also needed for the complete bridge/round-trip. Final gate: owner-reviewed pipeline graph, committed stage contracts, fixture-based end-to-end proof, explicit acceptance, then LIFEOS-011 only after all child proofs pass. All four canonical aggregate tasks are currently Draft and their planned artifacts/proofs are absent.

evidence.detail: The committed extract maps parser, buffer, and bridge atoms at lines 17-19, states the aggregate three-step Phase 1 framing at line 20, and distinguishes the database-hosted output proposal at line 21.
All nine atoms remain queued; questions grant no execution authority; repetition verifies no claim; raw external fulltext is not duplicated.

Rows define parser, redb buffer, and envctl/redb-to-PostgreSQL bridge, then classify INGEST-CLAIM-004 as an architecture proposal whose interfaces, prerequisites, rollback, and acceptance criteria are unspecified.

The three component rows require parser fixtures, redb transaction/crash-replay evidence, and bounded PostgreSQL/vector/recovery receipts; INGEST-CLAIM-004 specifically requires an owner-reviewed graph, stage contracts, fixture-based end-to-end proof, and explicit acceptance.
FOUNDATION-001 is Draft and requires one source package owner, pinned input, build gate, consumer map, shadow retirement, and installed proof for every required binary/runtime.

POSTGRES-004 is Draft and defines archive-first, non-mutating inventory/ingestion with AST parsing, stable identity, hashes, ordering, provenance, checkpoints, exactly-once behavior, recovery, and lossless reconstruction.

PGAUTH-002 is the draft task that requires every redb record class to have an owner, lifetime, durability requirement, identity, synchronization edge, crash-replay rule, recovery source, observability signal, and explicit PostgreSQL/CodeDB relationship.
POSTGRES-006, omitted from the aggregate claim refs, owns ordered, idempotent, restartable, observable redb buffering/flush and prevents redb from becoming competing macro authority.

STORE-001 requires an explicit David-approved decision assigning data classes, synchronization direction, durability, recovery, and materialization; it explicitly says NotebookLM repetition is corroboration only.

POSTGRES-008 is Draft and defines deterministic projection from a selected PostgreSQL branch/version into disposable directory/build output; it is reverse materialization, not the forward redb-to-PostgreSQL bridge.

POSTGRES-009, omitted from the aggregate claim refs, requires a bounded source-to-database-to-projection-to-build-to-runtime round trip with byte, identity, behavior, provenance, rollback, and rebuild proofs.
LIFEOS-011 is Draft and permits final release acceptance only after all child graphs/proofs pass, including Ubuntu-first runtime, rollback, and reproducibility gates.

The proof records the exact source/fulltext SHA-256, structured-extract SHA-256, task-graph SHA-256, exact source-ID/registry mapping, all task refs existing, nine queued claims, and zero ready claims.
The proof summary says the source was captured as nine unresolved atoms and the gate verifies preservation/classification/mapping/queueing, not implementation or owner acceptance.
The ledger records NBSOURCE-030 proof status pass with SHA-256 a0ae8381ea30783229fe70774af9dc8664cec0578876a5fd1c1e1151448718bc, matching the current file hash.

missing_evidence: The exact 810-byte raw NBSOURCE-030 fulltext is not retained in the current repository; only its SHA-256, byte count, captured extract, claim table, and proof record are local. Direct independent re-reading of the original wording would require the exact fulltext or validated retrieval packet.
The queue-required owner-reviewed pipeline graph, committed stage-level contract artifacts, fixture-based end-to-end proof, and explicit acceptance decision do not exist yet.
All planned artifacts and proofs for FOUNDATION-001, POSTGRES-004, POSTGRES-008, and LIFEOS-011 are absent, and all four tasks are effectively Draft.

Stage 2 needs an explicit committed owner/authority linkage to PGAUTH-002, POSTGRES-006, and STORE-001; the aggregate claim's four canonical refs alone do not cover redb schema, durability, replay, or authority.

Stage 3 needs a committed distinction between forward redb-to-PostgreSQL ingestion and POSTGRES-008's reverse PostgreSQL-to-directory projection, plus bounded transaction/vector/recovery and round-trip evidence.
No parser, redb writer, envctl bridge, embedding, PostgreSQL transaction, projection, or installed-runtime execution trace was run or proven in this read-only architecture-framing verification.

claims.conclusion: Current local canonical records support the narrow statement that NBSOURCE-030 was captured and normalized as proposing a flat-file-to-database-hosted codebase representation. They do not establish that the proposal is implementable. The canonical claim row explicitly calls it a source-only proposal without a canonical file manifest, byte-level round trip, or ownership decision; the verification row remains queued; all four referenced implementation/policy tasks are Draft; and every named task-family, plan, acceptance, and proof artifact for POSTGRES-004, POSTGRES-008, POSTGRES-009, and PGAUTH-004 is absent. In addition, the exact 810-byte source fulltext is identified and hashed but not retained in the inspected workspace, so the semantic wording is independently evidenced only through the canonical normalized capture, not through locally reviewable raw source text.

All nine atoms remain queued; repetition is relationship evidence only; raw external fulltext is not duplicated.

NBV-362 is still queued and requires a canonical source manifest, schema/migration receipt, deterministic round trip, and owner-approved authority matrix.
The indexed source is marked captured-unverified and records assertions/questions only; the relation row says repetition closes no claim.

Draft PGAUTH-004 requires canonical source identity, selected branch/version, deterministic materializer, checksum/metadata contract, permitted edit surface, cleanup rule, and proof that projected output cannot become a competing writable owner.

Draft POSTGRES-004 requires stable identity, content/metadata checksums, ordering, checkpoints, exactly-once behavior, recovery, and lossless reconstruction for every user-space class; live ingestion and product implementation are blocked.

Draft POSTGRES-008 requires deterministic reconstruction of paths, bytes, metadata, ordering, permissions, and module relationships, with byte-identical repeat projections traceable to database identities.
Draft POSTGRES-009 requires a bounded source-to-database-to-projection-to-build-to-running-UDS round trip with byte, identity, behavior, provenance, rollback, and rebuild proof.

claims.conclusion: Refuted as a claim about the current or installed implementation. At nu_plugin HEAD 9451b20 and at the Yazelix-locked installed-source revision 532a461, nu_plugin_codedb has no codedb-store-redb/redb dependency and its scan/import commands return Nushell rows; the source and tests characterize the plugin surface as read-only. A real durable redb write exists, but in the separate codedb CLI/codedb_store_redb path, where persist_batch performs the transaction and commit. No benchmark target, benchmark dependency, percentile metric, warm/cold protocol, sync-mode matrix, concurrency matrix, or failure-injection latency result exists. Thus there is neither an exact plugin-to-redb operation nor primary benchmark evidence supporting microsecond latency.

evidence.detail: The canonical extract says source wording proves no implementation or performance claim, then records PERF-CLAIM-001 only as the source assertion.
The canonical row marks the claim source-only, unverified, and non-reproducible; measurement conditions are absent.

The queued requirement demands the exact plugin-to-redb benchmark, fixed payloads, warm/cold state, sync mode, concurrency, percentiles, failure injection, raw distributions, environment manifest, and durability semantics; all result/receipt fields are empty.
The plugin depends on codedb-cargo, codedb-core, codedb-rust-static, Nu protocol crates, serde_json, sha2, and toml; it has no codedb-store-redb or redb dependency.
codedb scan is explicitly described as read-only; although the signature accepts a store flag, run only computes scan_summary_rows and returns rows, with no store write.
codedb envctl import inventory accepts a store flag but run only converts the inventory to rows and returns them; the flag is not read and redb is not called.
The plugin test states and checks that the registered envctl-db commands are read-only and expose no mutating apply command.
The actual redb source-blob operation is in the separate store crate: open database, begin write, insert blob/file/metadata rows, then commit.
CaptureBatcher documents one Database::open and one durable fsync commit per batch and delegates persist_batch to the store helper; this is not a Nushell plugin call path.
The codedb CLI, not nu_plugin_codedb, selects/opens the store and calls BlobStore::persist_batch for each durable batch.
Existing store tests cover durability and resume behavior with tiny fixtures but record no elapsed times, distributions, percentiles, state matrix, sync-mode matrix, or failure-injection latency.
The revision locked by Yazelix for the installed package has the same plugin dependency boundary: no codedb-store-redb/redb dependency.
The install-owner input pins FlexNetOS/nu_plugin revision 532a461841316a6623701f9e206b97cafa994d40.
The profile exposes bundled codedb and nu_plugin_codedb binaries from one codedb-runtime-tools 0.1.0 store path; this proves package identity, not plugin-to-redb behavior or latency.

Read-only git tree/grep inspection of the exact installed-source revision found no benchmark directories/files and no criterion/divan/iai dependency or microsecond/latency/percentile/warm/cold/sync-mode benchmark terms.
envctl's redb use is its migration automation event store with typed API-layer constraints, not evidence of a nu_plugin CodeDB cache write or its latency.

PGAUTH-002, STORE-001, and POSTGRES-004 remain Draft policy cells: they require owner classification and claim-scoped benchmark evidence while blocking live store mutation/product implementation in planning.

missing_evidence: A real, pinned nu_plugin_codedb call path that invokes the redb writer; current and installed-source plugin code has no such dependency/call.
A versioned benchmark harness for the exact plugin-to-redb boundary rather than the separate codedb CLI/store helper.
Fixed payload corpus with exact byte sizes, schema/version identity, and deterministic record hashes.
Warm and cold database/filesystem/cache-state definitions and raw run manifests.
Explicit redb durability/sync modes, including whether commit/fsync is included in the measured interval.
Concurrency matrix for plugin invocations, writers, and readers, with contention/queueing policy.
Predeclared p50/p95/p99 (and preferably max) targets plus raw per-sample distributions and confidence/repetition methodology.
Failure injection covering commit errors, process interruption, disk-full/I/O faults, lock contention, malformed payloads, replay/retry, and durability verification.
Pinned hardware, kernel, filesystem, Nix closure, Nu/plugin versions, CPU governor, and environment manifest sufficient for independent reproduction.
Installed-runtime Nushell execution of the exact operation was not performed because the required operation is absent and any redb benchmark would mutate state, violating this read-only pass.

claims.conclusion:
The exact authority claim is refuted by current local primary evidence. PostgreSQL is an implemented optional direct CodeDB backend, but envctl is not the synchronizing writer in that path: CodeDB selects either redb (the default durable backend) or PostgreSQL and calls the selected backend's persist_batch directly. The PostgreSQL implementation performs its own INSERT/ON CONFLICT transaction into the dedicated codebase_codedb table and deliberately avoids the production codebase table. Envctl manages the PostgreSQL/RuVector component lifecycle and consumes already-embedded CodeDB export rows into an in-memory index; the inspected envctl path neither generates those embeddings nor writes ingested blocks/embeddings to PostgreSQL. The planning graph expresses a future PostgreSQL macro-authority/projection design, but every canonical task cited by this claim is Draft, the required authority artifacts are absent, and the claim queue remains queued with Ready empty.

Authority matrix (current evidence):
Layer | Authorized write trace | Denied, absent, or unproven trace
file | CodeDB materialization may emit derived files from a selected store. | Capture is read-only on the scanned tree; source files are not an authorized capture write target.
redb | Default CodeDB capture target; persist_batch commits each batch durably. Envctl separately owns a migration-ledger redb, not an ingestion bridge. | Redb is not proven to be merely transient, and no redb-to-envctl-to-PostgreSQL flush path was found.
envctl | Starts/stops/verifies the PostgreSQL/RuVector component and builds an in-memory semantic index from exported rows that already carry vectors. | No current block-ingestion DML writer, embedding generator, or redb-to-PostgreSQL synchronizer was found. Reverse synchronization from projections is explicitly forbidden by the Draft PGAUTH-004 target contract.
embedding | Existing embedding_minilm values from CodeDB export may be loaded into envctl's in-memory RuVector index. | The inspected envctl path skips rows without vectors and does not generate or persist embeddings; CodeDB PgStore's current DML omits an embedding column.
PostgreSQL | CodeDB may directly select PgStore and UPSERT raw file blobs in one transaction into a dedicated table. | It is not proven as the sole/permanent authority: redb remains a durable default, PgStore avoids production codebase, the owner-approved authority contract is missing, and no installed write trace was permitted or available.

Authorized current trace: source file --fs::read--> CodeDB capture --open selected backend--> redb persist_batch/commit OR PgStore INSERT...ON CONFLICT/commit. Denied or absent claimed trace: source/redb --envctl read+embed+sync--> PostgreSQL. Planned target trace (not current proof): PostgreSQL branch/version --envctl one-way projection--> disposable directory/build input; reverse writes from projection to canonical state are forbidden.

evidence.detail: The extract says source wording proves no implementation or authority; lines 19 and 23 record the asserted envctl bridge and authority claim, not verification.
All nine atoms remain queued; repetition verifies nothing.

AUTH-CLAIM-001 is queued and requires a committed authority policy, identity/version receipts, transaction authorization traces, conflict/rollback tests, and owner approval; the evidence/status cells are empty.
PGAUTH-001 is Draft and proposes one PostgreSQL identity/write authority per durable macro-state class; it prohibits live PostgreSQL mutation and ingestion during planning.
PGAUTH-004 is Draft; it defines one-way provenance for derived projections and explicitly forbids reverse synchronization into canonical state and generated-runtime hand edits.
POSTGRES-006/008/009 are Draft targets for runtime-store boundaries, PostgreSQL-to-directory projection, and a future end-to-end proof. They do not establish current envctl ingestion authority.
CodeDB declares the scanned tree read-only and the selected store as the only write target; it recognizes PostgreSQL directly as an alternative to a redb file.
CodeDB itself opens PgStore for a PostgreSQL spec or CaptureBatcher for redb; envctl is not in this call path.

Redb is the default store. Capture reads source bytes, opens one selected store, and directly invokes store.persist_batch; mutation_policy states the scan is read-only and the store is the only write target.
The BlobStore contract gives redb and PostgreSQL the same durable batch-persistence and read/materialization semantics rather than defining redb only as a transient buffer.
PgStore is a direct CodeDB backend; its default dedicated table does not collide with production codebase.
The default connection path still points at the retired /home/flexnetos/lifeos root, the default table is codebase_codedb, and connect creates/updates that table without an embedding column.
PgStore directly UPSERTs module_path/content/sha256/metadata and commits the batch transaction; no envctl call or embedding write occurs.
The only inspected live redb/PostgreSQL parity test is ignored and opt-in; it is not default proof.
The test writes the same batch directly through redb and PgStore trait objects and explicitly avoids production/shared PostgreSQL tables.
Envctl's semantic consumer is an HNSW index over exported CodeDB rows that already carry embedding_minilm values.
ExportedRow accepts an optional pre-existing embedding vector.
Envctl parses exported JSONL, skips rows without vectors, and builds an in-memory index; it neither generates embeddings nor persists them to PostgreSQL.
Envctl's declared role is to bring an existing PostgreSQL/RuVector cluster into its managed lifecycle plane; this is not an ingestion-writer implementation.
The verify hook only queries extension/table/vector completeness.
Install/fix start PostgreSQL and remove stops it while preserving data; no block or embedding DML appears.
Envctl's redb dependency is documented as its migration database/ledger store, not a CodeDB ingestion cache bridge.
Embedding generation is described as flowing via other runtime components; ruvector-postgres is a server-side extension, explicitly not a PostgreSQL client library.

missing_evidence: Committed, owner-approved authority policy assigning each file/redb/envctl/embedding/PostgreSQL data class exactly once; the queue explicitly requires this and PGAUTH-001/PGAUTH-004 remain Draft.
Identity/version receipts and an implemented one-way synchronization contract showing exactly which component may write each record at each transition.
A current envctl source call path that reads CodeDB/redb blocks, generates embeddings, authorizes PostgreSQL DML, commits idempotently, and records rollback/conflict receipts. No such path was found.

The generated redb use-case matrix, runtime-store boundary plan, envctl materialization plan, PostgreSQL projection/build acceptance, and cited task-family JSON artifacts; all probed paths are absent.
Conflict, crash-replay, rollback, failback, and forbidden-dual-write tests for the claimed five-layer flow.
Installed envctl runtime proof: /home/flexnetos/.nix-profile/bin/envctl is missing, so there is no profile-owned envctl writer to inspect.
Live PostgreSQL read proof at the active Meta root: the data tree exists but no socket was available; the source default still targets missing /home/flexnetos/lifeos/var/lib/postgresql.
Installed end-to-end write proof was intentionally not executed because this pass was strictly read-only; the existing live parity test is ignored, opt-in, and mutates a temporary PostgreSQL table.

A fresh nu_plugin code-intelligence index: the available index was branch-null/stale and did not resolve current backend symbols, so relationships were verified from exact current source lines instead.

claims.conclusion: Qualified, not verified as written. Current envctl implements a narrower deterministic filesystem-to-catalog and catalog-to-generated-config projection, including path-traversal refusal and repeatable output hashes. It does not currently select a PostgreSQL branch/version or reconstruct arbitrary database-hosted source files. The NotebookLM statement is retained only as a target architecture relationship under POSTGRES-008/009, not current behavior.

evidence.detail: The evidence boundary says source wording proves no implementation, authority, readiness, or performance claim; ENVPROJ-CLAIM-001 is classified only as an architecture proposal.
All seven atoms remain queued for primary evidence or decision, and repetition verifies no claim.
The canonical claim row labels the statement source-only and unverified and requires the selected-identity, byte, metadata, ordering, permissions, traceability, cleanup, and rebuild proof.
NBV-367 is still queued with the required bounded database-to-directory round-trip resolution.
CUR-044 records all NBSOURCE-031 atoms, including branch projection and raw-code assembly, as unresolved and requiring primary evidence.

POSTGRES-008 defines the desired PostgreSQL-to-directory invariants and POSTGRES-009 defines the bounded end-to-end proof, but both rows are Draft; POSTGRES-008 also excludes live materialization and product implementation from this planning scope.
The current catalog explicitly absorbs TOML/YAML/JSON/Rust/handoff files into in-memory rows; DB-first behavior is deferred to later slices.

CodeDB import rows are built by reading a Yazelix inventory and local target files, hashing readable bytes, and recording metadata. This is filesystem-to-catalog ingestion, not PostgreSQL-to-directory reconstruction.
Current rendering emits a fixed catalog/config file set plus JSON/TSV table projections; it does not reconstruct arbitrary database-hosted source files from module paths and ordered code blocks.
Projection writes create parent directories, write prepared bytes, and reject absolute or parent-traversal paths, but no database identity, block ordering, or permission restoration is involved.

A source-level test compares rendered tree hashes across two output directories and asserts deterministic fixed projections. It is not a database round trip and was not executed in this read-only pass.
The envctl db index is a deterministic std::fs scan of current roots, sorted by absolute path and content hash; it is not a PostgreSQL-backed source selector or projector.
DbSnapshot is built by scanning the filesystem into file and symbol indexes held in memory.
The semantic lane consumes externally emitted codedb export JSONL into an in-memory HNSW index behind a default-off feature.
from_export_jsonl parses module paths and vectors; it neither queries PostgreSQL nor writes reconstructed source files.
The only PostgreSQL-related workspace dependency is explicitly a server-side pgrx extension, not a client library.
The managed PostgreSQL component verifies extension and table presence plus embedding completeness; it defines no branch selection or materialization operation.

missing_evidence: A committed projection contract that explicitly defines the claimed four ordered steps, standard output-directory contract, branch/version identity selection, source schema, block ordering, path normalization/collision/traversal rules, permissions policy, and failure semantics.

The six artifacts referenced by POSTGRES-008 and POSTGRES-009 are absent: generated/task_families/POSTGRES-008.json, generated/envctl_materialization_plan.json, proof_records/POSTGRES-008.proof.json, generated/task_families/POSTGRES-009.json, generated/postgres_projection_build_acceptance.json, and proof_records/POSTGRES-009.proof.json.
Current envctl product code and tests that connect to PostgreSQL as a client, select an authorized branch/version, fetch stored paths/raw code/metadata, and reconstruct arbitrary source files.
A bounded source fixture plus database ingestion receipt and immutable database identities/version receipt.
Byte-for-byte input/database/output parity evidence, including encoding, duplicate/partial-block handling, deterministic ordering, metadata/module relationships, and repeated projection tree hashes.
Permission and executable-bit preservation evidence tied to the database metadata and reconstructed files.
Trace/audit receipts that connect every output path and hash back to selected database row/block identities.
Failure-injection and cleanup evidence proving partial outputs are removed and the database/source remain unchanged.
Delete-and-rebuild evidence proving disposable output can be reconstructed identically from the same selected database identity.
POSTGRES-009 end-to-end receipts for projection through profile-owned Yazelix/Nix build, execution, LifeOS UDS behavior, rollback, and rebuild.
A profile-owned installed envctl frontdoor and concrete installed-runtime projection output; the only executable found is a symlink into the raw repo target/release build.
A current non-mutating test receipt at the cited envctl and LifeOS commits; tests were not run because this task forbade builds/tests that could create generated files.

claims.conclusion: At envctl commit fd3d423fff767b4b96f67fdc46a8aac93da21559, the implemented `catalog render` path does not query PostgreSQL or select a production branch. CLI dispatch calls `Engine::catalog_render`, which calls `catalog::render`; `render` builds its snapshot by scanning repo/manifest files and the in-memory Registry, then writes deterministic projections. The current render request/report has no branch identity, database version, selector authority, authorization, audit-event, or rollback fields. PostgreSQL material found locally is limited to an optional, default-off server-side RuVector extension declaration and separate health/embedding-count psql probes; neither participates in projection. LifeOS primary tables explicitly mark the NotebookLM wording as source-only/unverified and queued, while POSTGRES-008 remains a Draft definition task. No matching integration test exists in current test directories, and no installed `envctl` binary/profile entry was available, so installed behavior was not proven. Owner decision and the complete selection/authorization/pinning/failure/audit/rollback contract remain absent.

evidence.detail: The canonical extract states that source wording proves no implementation or authority claim, then records ENVPROJ-CLAIM-002 as the PostgreSQL production-branch assertion.
All seven atoms remain queued for primary evidence or owner decision; repetition verifies no claim.
The row labels this claim source-only and unverified, specifically noting that no branch schema, selector, authority, invocation trace, or cited passage is supplied.
The canonical queue keeps the claim queued and requires owner decision plus source/integration-test tracing of query, authorization, version pinning, failure, audit, and rollback.
POSTGRES-008 says to define envctl projection from a selected PostgreSQL branch/version and is still Draft; its no-go scope prohibits live materialization and product implementation.
The module explicitly says the first slice is in-memory, accepts TOML/YAML/JSON/Rust registry/.handoff file inputs, and leaves DB-first behavior to later slices.
`scan` reads repo and manifest files, the lock, registry, profile/layout/agent files, and CodeDB import metadata; no PostgreSQL query or branch selection occurs.
`render` validates filesystem paths, calls the local-file `scan`, stabilizes that snapshot, and passes it to `render_projections`; this contradicts the claimed pre-projection PostgreSQL branch query.
`Engine::catalog_render` supplies only repo root, manifest directory, output directory, and optional target root to `catalog::render`.
The `CatalogCmd::Render` branch calls the same `catalog_engine::render`/`engine.catalog_render` path; there is no database or production-branch selector argument.

Render spec/report fields cover filesystem roots, generated files, source tables, bytes, and hashes; they contain no branch identity, version pin, selector authority, authorization, audit event, or rollback receipt.
The result records paths, source tables, byte counts, SHA-256 hashes, and a non-mutating-repo flag, then returns; no database-selection audit event or rollback token is emitted.

The inline unit test proves deterministic output across two local fixture directories. It does not create/query PostgreSQL, select a production branch, authorize selection, pin a database version, audit, or rollback.
The covered failure is only refusal to render inside the source repo, not database/selection failure.
The only PostgreSQL workspace package is explicitly described as a default-off pgrx server-side extension, not a client library.
`ruvector-postgres` is optional and enabled only by the separate `ruvector-pg` feature; default features are empty.
The managed PostgreSQL component performs health checks for extension presence, table counts, and embedding completeness; it does not select a production branch or project files.
The only local psql test located is explicitly read-only and queries codebase row/embedding counts, not branch selection or projection.

missing_evidence: Owner-approved identity for the production branch and explicit authority deciding which branch/version is production.
A PostgreSQL schema/table/view for branch identities and immutable selected versions.
The exact SQL selection query and its local envctl call path.
Authentication and authorization/RLS evidence for selection.
Pinned PostgreSQL, schema, extension, model, and selected database-version identifiers tied to projection output.
Fail-closed behavior for unavailable, ambiguous, unauthorized, stale, or invalid branch/version selection.
A durable audit event/receipt linking selector identity, actor, branch, version, query/result, and projection hashes.
Rollback behavior that restores a prior selected version and proves deterministic re-projection.
A local integration test exercising PostgreSQL selection through projection, including authorization, failure, audit, and rollback cases.
Installed-runtime proof: `envctl` was absent from PATH and `/home/flexnetos/.nix-profile/bin/envctl`, and no matching Nix profile element was present.
Referenced task-family JSON and proof records for PGAUTH-001, POSTGRES-005, and POSTGRES-008 were not materialized locally.

claims.conclusion: Current primary evidence contradicts the claim. In envctl, the only tracked use of `module_path` is the default-off RuVector semantic consumer: it parses exported JSONL and stores `module_path` as an HNSW vector id/metadata, not as an in-memory directory tree. The actual file reconstruction implementation is owned by CodeDB in the independent `nu_plugin` repository: `codedb materialize` lists stored `SourceFileRow.relative_path` rows, joins each path directly to `out_dir`, and writes files to disk. PostgreSQL happens to store that relative path in its `module_path` column, but the materializer is CodeDB, not envctl, and it does not reconstruct an exact directory structure in memory. The broader exact-reconstruction wording is also false: there is no materialization-time path normalization or traversal/absolute-path refusal; duplicate keys update/overwrite rather than refuse; directories are inferred only as file parents (empty directories are not restored); symlinks are capture gaps; batch capture records no file mode; and tests cover checksum/byte round trips, one executable-bit case, and backend path/hash parity, not exact whole-tree reconstruction. CodeDB export rows that name envctl as a possible materialization owner are declarative (`runtime_status=declared`) and have no current envctl consumer. Relevant tracked source files had empty diffs. During the read-only pass, unrelated concurrent changes appeared under envctl `home/.claude/`; `nu_plugin` already had untracked `.claude/`. No repository or runtime was edited by this verification.

evidence.detail: The canonical claim row itself labels the assertion source-only/unverified and states that no metadata schema, path-safety policy, collision rule, traversal guard, source path, or test was supplied.

POSTGRES-008 is Draft and defines desired deterministic reconstruction of paths, bytes, metadata, ordering, permissions, and relationships. Its mutation boundary forbids live materialization/product implementation, so it cannot prove a current envctl implementation.

`ExportedRow.module_path` is loaded with an embedding and inserted as a RuVector/HNSW entry id and metadata. The in-memory object is `SemanticIndex`, not a directory tree, and no path operations occur.
Tests prove semantic top-k ordering and vector dimension errors only. They do not materialize paths, files, directories, permissions, or stored bytes.

CodeDB emits `codedb_materialization_targets` rows that name envctl as an explicit-request owner, but marks that row only `declared_and_checksum_bound`; the proven blob round trip is owned by `codedb_store_redb`.

The CodeDB-produced runtime-integration row says envctl consumes exports, forbids envctl redb access, and sets `runtime_status` to `declared`; it is metadata describing a desired boundary, not an envctl implementation.

`codedb materialize` opens a CodeDB `BlobStore`, lists files, directly computes `out_dir.join(file.relative_path)`, delegates one-file disk materialization, and verifies SHA-256. It builds no in-memory directory structure and performs no path validation in this loop.
evidence.relationship: actual materialization owner and call path

evidence.detail: The backend-neutral contract is keyed by `SourceFileRow.relative_path`; `list_source_files` returns rows in key order and `materialize_source_file` writes one output path, restoring a stored Unix mode when present.
PgStore documents its table shape: `module_path` is unique and paired with byte content/checksum/metadata. The default is the dedicated `codebase_codedb` table, not production `codebase`.
Persist maps each input `relative_path` into PostgreSQL `module_path` and uses `ON CONFLICT (module_path) DO UPDATE`; collisions overwrite content/checksum/metadata instead of being refused.

PgStore orders rows lexically with `ORDER BY module_path`, maps them back to `relative_path`, creates parent directories, writes bytes to disk, optionally chmods when metadata contains a mode, and rechecksums the file.
PostgreSQL batch metadata explicitly records `permission_capture=gap_not_available_for_raw_blob`; therefore batch-captured files cannot guarantee exact permission reconstruction.

Batched redb persistence inserts by raw relative-path key, overwriting an existing key, and records the same permission-capture gap. There is no collision refusal or normalized-path equivalence check.
The redb full-tree read walks the source-files table in key order and emits file rows; it does not emit an explicit directory tree.

Redb materialization creates only a file's parent directories, writes bytes, optionally restores a parseable Unix mode, and rechecksums the output. The caller-supplied output path is not containment-checked.
Filesystem capture sorts entries by `relative_path`, providing deterministic source scan order.

Capture-time path conversion only strips the root prefix when possible, falls back to the original path otherwise, and changes backslashes to slashes. It does not reject absolute paths or `..` components.

Capture counts directories but persists only regular non-symlink files; symlinks and special entries become gaps. Consequently empty directories and symlink topology cannot be exactly reconstructed by the blob materializer.
Tests prove a single source blob survives backup/restore and that one binary artifact materializes byte-for-byte with matching SHA-256; they are not exact whole-tree reconstruction tests.
One Unix-only test proves executable-bit restoration for a file captured through the per-file path, not through batch capture or PostgreSQL batch persistence.
The ignored live test compares only `(relative_path, sha256)` and captured-path sets between PostgreSQL and redb.
Assertions prove set equality and row count, not materialized bytes, path containment, collision refusal, directories, symlinks, or permissions.

missing_evidence: No current envctl source symbol, dependency, command, or call path consumes `codedb_materialization_targets` or invokes CodeDB `BlobStore` materialization.
No materialization-time normalization/containment function rejects absolute paths, `..` traversal, or normalized-path aliases before `out_dir.join(relative_path)`.
No collision-refusal policy exists for duplicate or normalization-equivalent paths; current PostgreSQL and redb persistence updates/overwrites the raw key.

No exact whole-tree reconstruction test covers nested files plus empty directories, symlinks, deterministic directory creation order, path containment, collisions, exact bytes, and permissions together.
No permission-complete batch path exists: both redb and PostgreSQL batch metadata explicitly record that permission capture is unavailable.
Installed runtime behavior was not invoked or claimed; source evidence was sufficient to refute the current-implementation wording, so active-profile runtime materialization proof remains absent.

claims.conclusion: Current primary evidence contradicts the claim. envctl has no raw_code block schema, block identity, ordering key, concatenation path, or Rust-file projection. Its current catalog is explicitly an in-memory/file-first bridge whose renderer emits a fixed set of generated catalog/config files (including two TOML projections, but no Rust source), and its optional CodeDB semantic consumer deserializes only module_path plus embedding_minilm while ignoring extra fields. The exact-byte file materializer found in the workspace belongs to CodeDB/nu_plugin, and it restores whole SHA-256-addressed source blobs rather than concatenating raw_code blocks. CodeDB's exported envctl integration records say envctl's role is declared/downstream; they do not implement the consumer in envctl. Existing envctl tests cover deterministic fixed catalog projections and secret redaction for catalog environment rows, not raw-block reconstruction, duplicate/partial-block behavior, encoding, transactional failure cleanup, or Rust/TOML byte parity. No profile-owned envctl frontdoor exists; an explicit workspace-owned binary was inspected read-only and exposed catalog tables with zero codedb_file_imports rows for the envctl repo.

evidence.detail: Records the exact current-implementation claim and its canonical task references; this is the assertion being checked, not implementation proof.
States that all atoms remain queued for primary evidence and repetition verifies no claim.
Canonical row labels the assertion source-only/unverified and identifies the absent block schema, ordering key, byte-parity trace, output fixture, and cited passage.
POSTGRES-008 is Draft policy work to define deterministic database-to-directory projection; its acceptance criteria describe desired future proof rather than current implementation.
The catalog declares itself in-memory and non-mutating, keeps TOML/YAML/JSON/Rust registries as file inputs, and says later slices add DB-first behavior.
The only CodeDB export row type in envctl contains module_path and embedding_minilm; no raw_code, block identity, file identity, or ordering field exists.
The consumer parses JSONL solely to build semantic vector rows, skips vectorless rows, and never materializes source bytes.
CatalogSnapshot has normalized tables and codedb_file_imports but no raw-code block collection or source-file reconstruction table.
CodedbFileImportRow carries path, hash/blob metadata, and structured key/value rows; it does not carry source blob bytes or raw_code blocks.
envctl reads current files to calculate hashes, lengths, blob_ref labels, and structured rows, then stores metadata in the in-memory snapshot; it does not persist or concatenate raw source blocks.
Structured rows are parsed config values or nonblank text lines. Rust is not an accepted parser_hint here, and line flattening is not an exact-byte file reconstruction mechanism.

The renderer constructs a fixed list of generated outputs from a CatalogSnapshot. It emits manifest/components.catalog.toml and .codex/config.toml, plus YAML/JSON/shell/KDL/systemd/table projections; it emits no .rs file and consumes no raw_code blocks.

Projection writes create parents and call std::fs::write directly. This path has relative/parent traversal guards but no staging transaction, rollback, or cleanup of earlier files after a later failure.
The deterministic-repeat test compares two fixed catalog render trees and enumerates TOML/config outputs; it does not test Rust source or multi-block raw_code reconstruction.
Catalog hook environment values with sensitive keys are redacted. This is a real but narrower secret guard and is not a guard over reconstructed source/raw_code output.
The PostgreSQL RuVector dependency is explicitly a default-off server-side extension, not a client library, so it does not implement a PostgreSQL raw-code projection reader.
CodeDB owns blob semantics and code facts; envctl is specified only as a downstream consumer of exports/checksums and must not read redb internals.
The contract assigns blob refs/source rows/checksums to CodeDB and says source blobs are restored by SHA-256 before envctl consumes materialization rows; it does not define raw_code concatenation.
CodeDB emits checksum-bound materialization target rows naming envctl as owner, while the proven materializer is codedb_store_redb blob restoration.
The exported runtime integration row says envctl should consume exports/materialize on request but sets runtime_status to declared.
The implemented capture/materialize CLI belongs to codedb and stores/re-emits complete regular-file bytes as content-addressed blobs; it is not envctl and does not concatenate blocks.
codedb lists source files, asks its store to materialize each whole file, and verifies SHA-256 round-trip. No raw-code block identity or ordering exists in this path.
The store resolves relative path to a SHA-256 blob, writes the complete stored byte vector, restores Unix mode, and rechecksums the file.
Raw source bytes are blobs with hashes and policy rows; queryable rows point to blobs and do not replace exact bytes.
CodeDB tests exact Rust-source bytes after backup/restore and exact binary bytes, confirming whole-blob reconstruction under CodeDB rather than envctl raw_code concatenation.

missing_evidence: An implemented envctl consumer for CodeDB/PostgreSQL materialization rows with a concrete raw_code block schema, stable file/block identity, and total ordering key; current source has none.
A primary test fixture that reconstructs both a Rust source file and Cargo.toml from multiple raw_code blocks and asserts exact byte equality, including BOM/newline/invalid-UTF-8 behavior.
Tests for duplicate block identity, duplicate ordering keys, missing/partial blocks, cross-file collisions, and deterministic tie/refusal behavior.
A secret-output policy specifically applied to source/blob/raw_code materialization. Existing envctl catalog redaction covers sensitive environment values, not arbitrary source bytes.
Atomic staging and failure-cleanup tests proving no partial output tree remains after a mid-projection write/checksum/permission failure; current render/store paths write files directly.

An executed current test receipt for the claimed mechanism. No cargo/build/materialization test was run because this pass prohibited generated files and runtime writes; only source tests and a read-only catalog-tables command were inspected.

A profile-owned active envctl binary and concrete profile-runtime output. /home/flexnetos/.nix-profile/bin/envctl was absent; only /home/flexnetos/meta/usr/bin/envctl -> src/envctl/target/release/envctl was available for explicit read-only inspection.

claims.conclusion: Qualified, not verified as an end-to-end handoff. Current envctl separately supports deterministic catalog rendering and build-system detection/execution, including a generic Nix-flake build command. Yazelix separately exposes a profile-owned Nix package interface. No current call path connects a selected database projection to that build path, so the full projected-directory-to-Yazelix/Nix claim remains target architecture rather than installed behavior.

evidence.detail: The canonical extract states that source wording proves no implementation/readiness and classifies ENVPROJ-CLAIM-005 as an architecture proposal.
All seven NBSOURCE-031 atoms remain queued for primary evidence or owner decision; repetition verifies no claim.
The row explicitly says source-only/unverified and names the missing build input, flake target, Yazelix owner, build command, closure, and reproducible result.
ENVPROJ-CLAIM-005 is still queued and requires the disposable build-input boundary plus source, output, closure, behavior, provenance, and rollback receipts.
POSTGRES-008 and POSTGRES-009 define the intended disposable projection and end-to-end Yazelix/Nix proof, but both rows are Draft and point to proof/acceptance artifacts that are not present.
FOUNDATION-003 is a Draft source-to-package-to-profile-to-runtime proof plan, not a completed receipt.
POSTGRES-008 and POSTGRES-009 have effective status draft; proof ledger line, observed_at, revision, and sha256 are all null.
FOUNDATION-003 is draft with null proof metadata.

The canonical conflict ledger says NBSOURCE-031 supplies no independent local source, installed-runtime, benchmark, authority, release, readiness, or citation proof and must remain verification-gated.
The add-repo flow detects a BuildPlan and executes its command in clone_dir. This proves a generic clone-to-build path, not a PostgreSQL/CodeDB projected-directory handoff.
The detector prioritizes native signals and puts flake.nix last, so a Rust tree with Cargo.toml is not automatically handed to Nix.
A flake-only clone can select NixFlake and run `nix build --no-link --print-out-paths .`; detection consumes clone_dir and has no projection/database identity input.
Catalog render writes deterministic generated configuration projections outside the source repo and records per-file hashes/provenance, but this function does not invoke a compiler, Nix, or Yazelix.
Tests prove deterministic catalog outputs and refusal of an output path inside the repo; they do not test database reconstruction or a build handoff.
catalog_render and add_repo/connect_repo are distinct engine entrypoints; no handoff from CatalogRenderReport/out_dir into AddRepoSpec/build execution appears in these APIs.
The current CodeDB consumer accepts exported JSONL containing module_path and embedding_minilm; extra fields are ignored. It is a semantic index input, not raw_code reconstruction.
from_export_jsonl builds an in-memory semantic index from module_path/vector rows and does not materialize files.
The catalog CodeDB importer reads an existing Yazelix file inventory and existing target files into metadata/blob rows; it does not project database rows back into a build directory.
mkYazelix can accept an optional src argument, which is a potential package boundary, but no envctl projection adapter or receipt contract is defined here.
Yazelix exposes packages and maintainer/CI dev shells; this alone does not prove envctl passed a projected directory to them.

The runtime contract distinguishes fixed packaged runtime, generated state, and maintainer-only source-checkout workflows, preventing generated runtime from being treated as the missing disposable build input.

readlink resolved the active frontdoor to /nix/store/rd29hc9xjbsv9p2i86q90k038j5glxvf-lifeos-foundation-yzx/bin/yzx, and the active profile element points to packages.x86_64-linux.lifeos_foundation_yzx from the local Yazelix path. This proves the frontdoor owner, not claim-scoped projected-build behavior.

missing_evidence: A committed disposable build-input contract identifying the selected database branch/version, projection namespace/path, allowed contents, permissions, lifetime, cleanup owner, and non-authority status.

Current envctl source and an integration test that reconstruct complete files from database identities/raw_code into that bounded directory and then pass that exact directory to a specific Nix flake target or Yazelix package interface.

A claim-scoped isolated build receipt containing the exact source/database identities, envctl invocation, build command, flake lock/input revisions, derivation/output path, output hashes, and full Nix closure/reference receipt.
A behavior receipt from the built output, including the required running LifeOS/UDS response and its linkage to the projected source identity.
Provenance and byte-parity receipts connecting database rows to projected files and projected files to the built output, including deterministic rebuild evidence.
Rollback receipts showing deletion of the bounded database namespace and derived projection/build outputs while proving original source and installed profile remained unchanged.

evidence.detail: Canonical row labels YNP-CLAIM-001 source-only and unverified, explicitly listing absent agent identities, plugin names, registration, command path, source/package owner, and runtime trace.
Queue requires pinned source/manifest, agent-to-command call path, registration receipt, tests, and profile-owned runtime trace.
The extract says all atoms remain queued, repetition verifies no claim, and installed proof must follow the Yazelix config/generated/profile ownership split.
`codedbNuPlugin` is pinned to GitHub `FlexNetOS/nu_plugin` revision `532a461841316a6623701f9e206b97cafa994d40` with a fixed narHash.
Pinned workspace manifest selects `nu-plugin` and `nu-protocol` 0.113.1.
Pinned manifest defines binary `nu_plugin_codedb` and depends on the Nushell plugin/protocol crates.
Current source imports `Plugin`, `PluginCommand`, `serve_plugin`, and `MsgPackSerializer` from `nu_plugin`.
`CodeDbPlugin::commands` exposes the native CodeDB command set to Nushell.
Binary entrypoint serves `CodeDbPlugin` with `MsgPackSerializer`.
Nix package uses Cargo.lock, builds/tests CodeDB plus `nu_plugin_codedb`, installs both binaries, and records their runtime paths.
Immutable installed metadata identifies the Nu runtime tool, source repository, pinned revision `532a461...`, commands, and `YAZELIX_CODEDB_PLUGIN_BIN` placement.

Resolved to `/nix/store/rd29...-lifeos-foundation-yzx/bin/yzx`; `yzx run nu --version` returned 0.113.1, `yzx run codedb --version` returned 0.1.0, and a transient immutable-HOME `yzx run nu --no-config-file --plugins .../nu_plugin_codedb -c 'codedb tables | to json'` returned structured table rows.
Using the same profile frontdoor against the actual HOME, `yzx run nu --no-config-file -c 'plugin list | to json'` returned `[]`.
Test builds the plugin, registers it only in a temporary HOME/plugin-config, invokes `plugin use codedb`, and asserts the real HOME registry is unchanged.
Test explicitly defines Yazelix readiness with CodeDB bridge paths present without plugin registration.
Enabled-mode smoke advertises CLI/plugin paths but requires `plugin_registry_exists == false`.
Current Codex bridge directs agent consumption through bounded `codedb` CLI calls, not a Nushell native-plugin invocation.
Alternative documented agent route is bounded read-only MCP via `codedb mcp serve`.

Generated initializer inventory contained only carapace, starship, yazelix extern/init, and zoxide files; exact search found no `nu_plugin_codedb`, `plugin use/add codedb`, `--plugins ... codedb`, or CodeDB plugin environment references.

missing_evidence: A named current agent identity and source/config-to-command call path that invokes `nu --plugins .../nu_plugin_codedb` or `plugin use codedb`.
A registration or transient-load receipt produced by an actual agent session; the active HOME registry currently reports `[]`.
A current generated-runtime initializer or other profile-owned automation path that loads the plugin for agents; the generated initializer search returned no match.

A current full test/CI execution receipt tied to the installed pin or current nu_plugin HEAD. Test sources and CI declarations exist, and a manual profile transient smoke passed, but the full suite was not run because this pass forbids generated/build files.

Installed-profile parity with current nu_plugin HEAD, or an owner-approved statement that the older installed pin `532a461...` remains the intended current runtime while repository HEAD is `9451b20...`.

claims.conclusion: Current local primary evidence proves the narrower concrete statement that `nu_plugin_codedb` is a Rust-native Nushell plugin binary: Cargo declares it as a Rust `bin`, its Rust entrypoint implements `nu_plugin::Plugin` and calls `serve_plugin(..., MsgPackSerializer)`, Nix builds and tests it through `rustPlatform.buildRustPackage` with a locked Cargo graph, and the active profile resolves it through the profile-owned Yazelix foundation closure to a verified ELF containing a rustc 1.95.0 marker. The installed binary SHA-256 is `149537bbba39583451d0c33c0313d7a40b618b41b5fa4fcdaefd3b7a613c5127`; its package NAR hash is `sha256-hctmPEcqDD3AS4cJfQSudDAb1LdlolsY0V9tplwFKfg=` and `nix-store --verify-path` succeeded. Qualification is required because NBSOURCE-032 never enumerates the plural set of "referenced Nushell plugins", so only `nu_plugin_codedb` could be provenance-resolved. Also, the active profile is pinned through Yazelix/Yazi assets to nu_plugin commit `532a461...`, while current nu_plugin source is `9451b20...`; the relevant Cargo target, Rust entrypoint, and Nix build definition are unchanged, but Cargo.lock changed. The generated tree under `~/.local/share/yazelix` has no codedb/plugin materialization, and no independent clean rebuild was performed in this read-only pass.

evidence.detail: The atomic map states YNP-CLAIM-002 in plural form and maps it to FOUNDATION-001, RECOVERY-002, and ADOPT-001.
The canonical row labels the claim source-only/unverified and requires Cargo targets, Nix derivation, binary output, closure, and profile resolution.
Current source defines package `nu_plugin_codedb`, an explicit `[[bin]]` named `nu_plugin_codedb` at `src/main.rs`, depending on the Rust crates `nu-plugin` and `nu-protocol`.
Workspace pins Rust 1.93.1 semantics, edition 2024, and nu-plugin/nu-protocol dependency requirements 0.113.1.
Cargo.lock fixes nu-plugin and nu-plugin-core at 0.113.1 with registry source and checksums; nu-plugin-core includes rmp-serde.
The lock records `nu_plugin_codedb` 0.1.0 and its nu-plugin/nu-protocol dependencies.
Rust source imports `nu_plugin` traits, `MsgPackSerializer`, and `serve_plugin`, defines `CodeDbPlugin`, and implements `Plugin`.
The Rust `main` serves `CodeDbPlugin` using `MsgPackSerializer`.
The package selects Cargo packages `codedb` and `nu_plugin_codedb` and uses `rustPlatform.buildRustPackage`.
The derivation consumes Cargo.lock, builds/tests both selected packages, requires the release plugin binary, and installs it executable to `$out/bin/nu_plugin_codedb`.
Checks are enabled and passthru metadata declares the bundled `nu_plugin_codedb` command and environment path.
Current Yazi-assets source pins the nu_plugin flake to commit 9451b20e42d79f72d4fa5b2a190a39bd1d9d5e5a.
The asset package consumes `codedb_runtime_tools`, links its `codedb` and `nu_plugin_codedb` binaries into the runtime tool tree, and copies package metadata.
The Nix install check requires executable codedb/plugin paths and plugin metadata.
Current child lock fixes the nu_plugin revision and NAR hash at 9451b20e.
The Yazelix lock used by the active profile pins codedbNuPlugin to older ancestor commit 532a461841316a6623701f9e206b97cafa994d40 with a NAR hash.
The same lock pins yazi-assets commit 5f36f391..., which consumes codedbNuPlugin.
Profile-owned metadata identifies the source repo/revision, package input/attr, runtime artifact, and plugin path.
The installed Nix package declares bundled commands and the exact `nu_plugin_codedb` store path.

Resolves through the profile-owned foundation closure to `/nix/store/9cym...-codedb-runtime-tools-0.1.0/bin/nu_plugin_codedb`; it is an x86-64 ELF PIE executable, size 17,151,672, with SHA-256 `149537...c5127`, and contains a `rustc version 1.95.0` marker plus Rust source/vendor paths.
Derivation JSON names `codedb-runtime-tools-0.1.0`, uses cargo/rustc 1.95.0 build inputs, release build flags `-p codedb -p nu_plugin_codedb`, tests enabled, and outputs the installed package.
Nix records and content verification confirms NAR hash `sha256-hctmPEcqDD3AS4cJfQSudDAb1LdlolsY0V9tplwFKfg=`; the package is present in the active foundation closure.
SHA-256 comparisons for Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock, plugin Cargo.toml/main.rs, Nix package definition, flake.nix, and flake.lock all exactly match git commit 532a461.

missing_evidence: NBSOURCE-032 does not enumerate the plural set of referenced Nushell plugins; only `nu_plugin_codedb` could be tied to a Cargo package, Nix derivation, closure, and active profile path.

No independent clean rebuild was run to demonstrate a second build reproducing the existing NAR/binary hashes; the read-only pass instead verified the already-realized store path and its registered content hash.

The current generated output tree `/home/flexnetos/.local/share/yazelix` contains no codedb or `nu_plugin_codedb` manifest/artifact, so generated-runtime materialization is not proven even though the active profile package and profile-owned runtime closure are proven.

The active profile still consumes nu_plugin commit `532a461...` through yazi-assets `5f36f391...`, while current child source pins `9451b20...`; current-head packaging was inspected but not realized in the active profile.

claims.conclusion: The concrete profile-owned `nu_plugin_codedb` path is proven to use MessagePack: Yazelix pins FlexNetOS/nu_plugin commit 532a461841316a6623701f9e206b97cafa994d40; that commit pins Nushell plugin/protocol crates 0.113.1 and calls `serve_plugin(&CodeDbPlugin, MsgPackSerializer)`. The locally cached Nushell source at upstream commit 9c05da49a89c8e7a898be3f712536087a415002a specifies the engine/plugin protocol, implements MessagePack encode/decode, and contains in-memory round-trip fixtures. The active profile resolves both `nu` and `nu_plugin_codedb`; `nu --version` is 0.113.1. A live `--stdio` capture began with the length-prefixed `msgpack` encoder name and decoded to `Hello { protocol: nu-plugin, version: 0.113.1 }`; an isolated installed-Nu invocation then completed a shell-to-plugin-to-shell command round trip and returned a structured CodeDB row. Qualification is required because Nushell 0.113.1 supports both JSON and MessagePack encoders, and NBSOURCE-032 does not identify the broader plural set of 'referenced Nushell plugins'; current primary evidence proves the profile-owned CodeDB plugin, not every unnamed plugin.

evidence.detail: YNP-CLAIM-003 was queued with the same primary-evidence requirements before this pass.
The committed codedbNuPlugin node pins FlexNetOS/nu_plugin revision 532a461841316a6623701f9e206b97cafa994d40 with narHash sha256-1DZ9WyAkSvuMmbPsO55S7qlwZHVuzpVdJ7EGCw1NZtk=.

The installed source pin declares nu-plugin = 0.113.1 and nu-protocol = 0.113.1; its Cargo.lock additionally pins nu-plugin-core/nu-plugin-protocol 0.113.1 and rmp-serde 1.3.1 with registry checksums.
The installed plugin source imports MsgPackSerializer and serve_plugin.
The plugin main entry point invokes `serve_plugin(&CodeDbPlugin, MsgPackSerializer)`.
The locally cached crate source records the Nushell upstream commit and path `crates/nu-plugin-core`.
The crate defines serialized engine/plugin protocol types and delegates byte-level encoding to nu_plugin_core.
PluginInput requires Hello(ProtocolInfo) as the first engine-to-plugin message.
PluginOutput requires Hello(ProtocolInfo) as the first plugin-to-engine message.
ProtocolInfo carries protocol identity, crate semantic version, and features; the default version comes from the 0.113.1 crate package.
MsgPackSerializer identifies itself as `msgpack`; both PluginInput and PluginOutput are encoded with rmp_serde::encode::write_named and decoded with rmp_serde::Deserializer.
EncodingType explicitly supports both Json and MsgPack and negotiates from `json` or `msgpack`; MessagePack is not universal to every Nushell plugin.
serve_plugin announces the selected encoding before wiring stdin/stdout or local-socket I/O.
The encoding handshake is one length byte followed by the encoder name; the live capture produced `07 6d73677061636b` (`msgpack`).
After creating the engine interface, the plugin sends its Hello message.
Pinned source contains in-memory signature and structured Run call encode/decode round-trip tests; msgpack.rs lines 110-114 instantiate the shared fixture for MsgPackSerializer.
Resolved to /nix/store/rd29hc9xjbsv9p2i86q90k038j5glxvf-lifeos-foundation-yzx/bin/yzx.
Resolved through lifeos-foundation-yzx to /nix/store/n46i1nngcwinyml4xys5gzvhf4lqp23d-nushell-0.113.1/bin/nu; live version output was 0.113.1.

Resolved to /nix/store/9cymn7a4kj55ckjmxsn3yx2krqwzcw0w-codedb-runtime-tools-0.1.0/bin/nu_plugin_codedb, SHA-256 149537bbba39583451d0c33c0313d7a40b618b41b5fa4fcdaefd3b7a613c5127; `--help` reported Encoder: msgpack.

Live bytes were `076d73677061636b81a548656c6c6f83...`; stripping the length-prefixed encoder marker and decoding the payload with installed Nu `from msgpack` yielded `{"Hello":{"protocol":"nu-plugin","version":"0.113.1","features":[{"name":"LocalSocket"}]}}`.

With HOME/XDG roots set to /nonexistent, `nu --no-config-file --plugin-config /dev/null --plugins ... -c 'codedb tables ...'` exited 0 and returned `{"table":"codedb_contexts","status":"planned","rows":0,"note":"identity context rows are not captured in CDB013"}`.

missing_evidence: NBSOURCE-032 does not identify the full plural set of 'referenced Nushell plugins'. Concrete pin, serializer call path, handshake capture, and round-trip proof were established only for the profile-owned `nu_plugin_codedb`; equivalent identity and protocol captures are missing for any other plugin the source intended to include.

The upstream source itself shows that Nushell accepts either JSON or MessagePack encoders. Evidence that every unnamed referenced plugin selects MessagePack would be required to upgrade this qualified result to verified at the source's broad plural wording.
