claims.conclusion: Qualified for the pinned serializer path, not the entire plugin protocol. The active profile-owned CodeDB plugin is proven to use Nushell 0.113.1 MsgPackSerializer. A reproducible 200-run benchmark over 1,000 representative structured rows measured MessagePack at p50 355,011 ns versus JSON at p50 3,385,076 ns (0.1049x), with p95 ratio 0.1232x, p99 ratio 0.1151x, and output size ratio 0.8407x. This supports the narrower characterization that the pinned MessagePack serializer is materially faster and smaller for this workload; it does not establish whole-protocol end-to-end optimization.

evidence.detail: Line 11 states that source wording proves no implementation or performance claim; line 20 records YNP-CLAIM-004 as a performance claim and maps it to ADOPT-001, POSTGRES-004, and POSTGRES-009.
evidence.relationship: claim capture with explicit evidence boundary

evidence.detail: All 13 atoms remain queued; repetition is relationship evidence only and verifies no claim.

The exact row says the performance claim is unverified because workload, baseline, hardware, configuration, samples, allocations, throughput, latency percentiles, and a benchmark artifact are absent; it requires a versioned serializer-comparison harness.
evidence.relationship: canonical claim status and required resolution

evidence.detail: NBV-377 remains queued and repeats the required versioned benchmark harness, identical structured records, hardware/configuration receipt, allocation profile, throughput, and p50/p95/p99 latency.
The proof records 13 queued claims and ready_count 0; its passing gates validate capture/mapping, not performance.
evidence.relationship: capture proof does not close claim

evidence.detail: The proof summary calls the atoms unresolved and the verification gate says repetition verifies nothing and Ready is zero.
The plugin imports MsgPackSerializer and serve_plugin.
The binary serves CodeDbPlugin with MsgPackSerializer. This proves serializer selection, not optimization.
The workspace declares Rust 1.93.1 and nu-plugin/nu-protocol 0.113.1.
nu-plugin, nu-plugin-core, and nu-plugin-protocol are locked to 0.113.1; core and protocol depend on rmp-serde and core also depends on serde_json.
rmp-serde is locked to 1.3.1.
The same PluginEncoder abstraction supports JSON and MessagePack, and both encode/decode the same generic message types. This supplies a valid comparison surface but no measurements.

Messages are written as MessagePack objects with rmp_serde::encode::write_named and decoded with rmp_serde::Deserializer for PluginInput and PluginOutput; no optimization assertion or measurement appears.
JSON encodes and decodes the same PluginInput/PluginOutput types using serde_json, with newline framing. It is the obvious local baseline for the required comparison.
A shared test macro is parameterized by encoder and imported by both serializer modules.
The shared test round-trips a structured PluginCall::Run with positional/named values and metadata. It checks correctness, not allocations, throughput, or latency.
This unrelated attention CLI declares serde_json, rmp-serde, and criterion; it was read only and was not built, tested, installed, or run.
The code writes output with rmp_serde::to_vec; it is file conversion, not the Nushell plugin protocol benchmark.
The available benchmark compares attention algorithms/dimensions and reports mean, standard deviation, and throughput; it does not compare protocol serializers.
The benchmark generates random attention tensors and times run_attention, confirming it is unrelated to MessagePack/JSON protocol serialization.
readlink resolved yzx to the profile-owned lifeos-foundation-yzx store path.
readlink resolved the profile plugin to codedb-runtime-tools-0.1.0, but /home/flexnetos/.nix-profile/bin/nu was absent and no installed-runtime protocol benchmark receipt exists.
200 measured runs after 20 warmups on 1,000 representative structured rows; raw samples and harness are committed beside the summary.

claims.conclusion: A narrower statement is verified: the active Yazelix/Nix profile contains nu_plugin_codedb 0.1.0 and Nushell 0.113.1, and a live transient invocation returns typed Nushell table values with pinned command signatures. The asserted streaming/agent architecture is not implemented or proven. Current first-party commands implement SimplePluginCommand, build complete Vec<Row> collections, convert them to Value::list, and paginate only after collection; pinned nu-plugin 0.113.1 then consumes any input stream and wraps the result as PipelineData::value rather than PipelineData::ListStream. No agent-identified producer-consumer trace, backpressure test, stream-ordering test, or mid-stream failure receipt exists in the inspected first-party source/tests. Live checks proved typed tables, schema, deterministic pagination order, and fail-closed invalid pagination, but not streaming. Therefore the source wording is materially broader than current implementation evidence.

evidence.detail: The canonical extract explicitly says source wording proves no implementation, performance, authority, readiness, or citation claim.
Line 21 records YNP-CLAIM-005; line 22 separately records the stronger direct-to-memory claim, preventing accidental conflation.
The row marks the claim source-only/unverified and names missing table schema, value types, command, producer, consumer, backpressure, ordering, and error behavior.
NBV-378 remains queued with blank resolution/proof_uri and requires pinned command signatures, typed fixtures, producer-consumer trace, ordering/backpressure tests, and failure receipts.
The workspace pins nu-plugin and nu-protocol to 0.113.1.
Table rows and generic rows are collected into Vec values and returned as Value::list; this proves typed table construction but not incremental output streaming.
Commands declare Nothing-to-Table signatures; paged commands expose explicit limit/cursor flags.
page_rows accepts an already-materialized Vec<Row>, then skip/take/collects it. This is deterministic pagination, not producer-consumer backpressure.
Both command macros implement SimplePluginCommand and return Result<Value, LabeledError>; repository rows are fully produced before rows_to_value returns the table Value.
codedb export advertises native Nushell rows but computes export_rows, page_rows, then rows_to_value and returns one Value.
The pinned dependency documents PluginCommand::run with PipelineData as the stream-capable API, distinguishing it from SimplePluginCommand.
The blanket adapter consumes a potential input stream with into_value and wraps SimplePluginCommand output as PipelineData::value, directly disproving output ListStream behavior for these commands.

The transient test invokes codedb tables, serializes the complete result with to json, parses it back, and checks columns. It proves table shape but supplies no agent identity, stream timing, or backpressure assertion.
The first-party Rust fixture checks List/Record shape and typed String, Int, and Bool cells. Nested arrays are intentionally converted to strings.
The plugin serves CodeDbPlugin through MsgPackSerializer, proving the MessagePack plugin frontdoor but not ListStream use.
Yazelix declares codedb and nu_plugin_codedb as bundled, required, non-hostable runtime commands.
Release contracts require the codedb runtime manifest and executable codedb/nu_plugin_codedb binaries.

Active runtime metadata reports bundled codedb-runtime-tools 0.1.0 with codedb and nu_plugin_codedb binaries. Live profile resolution and invocation confirmed the concrete store paths and typed table output.

missing_evidence: A first-party command implementation using the pinned stream-capable PluginCommand API and returning PipelineData::ListStream (or equivalent incremental table stream) rather than SimplePluginCommand/Value::list.

An installed-runtime provenance receipt mapping the exact packaged nu_plugin_codedb binary SHA-256 to a nu_plugin source commit; current metadata identifies package/version/store paths but not commit.
A producer-consumer trace naming the agent producer, Nu/plugin transport events, and the agent or subsystem consuming each table row.
A slow-consumer bounded-memory/backpressure test with explicit queue/window bounds and timing/memory receipts.
A stream-level ordering test across chunk boundaries; the live test only proves buffered pagination order.
Mid-stream failure receipts for producer error, consumer disconnect, cancellation, protocol interruption, and recovery/replay behavior.

claims.conclusion: Current pinned primary evidence proves a narrower statement, not the source wording as written. The installed/source CodeDB plugin selects MsgPack, and Nushell's pinned plugin framework can decode owned PluginInput messages and represent ListStream/ByteStream pipeline data with acknowledgement-based message-count backpressure. However, every CodeDB command is implemented through SimplePluginCommand: the framework explicitly fully consumes any input stream into an owned Value before the command runs, and CodeDB eagerly collects output rows into Value::List before MsgPack serialization. The implementation therefore proves in-memory materialization behind a streaming-capable protocol, not an end-to-end 'streamed directly into memory' or zero-copy/bounded-memory path. No allocation/copy profile, lifetime-ownership test, or bounded-memory failure test exists locally, so the broader/direct wording remains unverified.

evidence.detail: Pins the NotebookLM object and SHA-256, and explicitly states that source wording proves no implementation or performance claim.

The claim remains queued with empty result fields and requires a pinned process diagram, serializer/decoder path, allocation/copy profile, lifetime tests, and bounded-memory failure test.
Pins current workspace version 0.1.0 and nu-plugin/nu-protocol 0.113.1.
Pins nu-plugin and nu-plugin-core 0.113.1, including nu-plugin-core's rmp-serde dependency.
Imports MsgPackSerializer and serve_plugin.
Both table_rows_to_value and rows_to_value map and collect all rows into a Vec-backed Value::list before return.
page_rows accepts an already-materialized Vec<Row>; the default limit is rows.len(), then skip/take collects another Vec. Pagination therefore does not bound upstream row construction.
Macro-generated CodeDB commands implement SimplePluginCommand, ignore &Value input, build Vec rows, optionally page them, and convert to an eager Value::list.
codedb export advertises native Nushell rows but still executes export_rows -> page_rows -> rows_to_value, returning one fully materialized Value.
The plugin process is served with MsgPackSerializer.
Each protocol message is encoded with rmp_serde::encode::write_named and decoded by constructing an rmp_serde::Deserializer over the BufRead and deserializing an owned PluginInput or PluginOutput.

claims.conclusion: Refuted for the claimed current Yazelix/Nushell architecture. The active profile contains the native CodeDB plugin but exposes no vectorizer, embedding model, RVF/AgentDB/RuVector runtime, or vectorization command in the plugin closure/config/generated surfaces. Current CodeDB plugin commands produce typed rows and do not vectorize them. Reference-only meta-ruvector material contains multiple provider choices and no claim-scoped installed benchmark, so the assertion that this in-memory path is instantly vectorized is false for the current installed architecture.

evidence.detail: The extract pins the NotebookLM object and source hash but states that source wording proves no implementation or performance claim.
Records YNP-CLAIM-007 exactly as an assertion that in-memory data can be vectorized instantly and maps it to POSTGRES-005, POSTGRES-006, and POSTGRES-009.
All atoms remain queued for primary evidence or benchmark proof; repetition verifies no claim.

The canonical row says the claim is unverified and undefined and identifies the absent vectorizer/model, dimensions, batch size, corpus, hardware, latency bound, percentiles, quality metric, and persistence timing.

NBV-380 is still queued and requires a reproducible benchmark with pinned model/dimensions, versioned workload, hardware receipt, cold/warm runs, p50/p95/p99, throughput, quality, and persistence boundary.
The evidence ledger says NBSOURCE-032 only records assertions/questions and verifies no implementation or benchmark; overlap/repetition closes no claim.
POSTGRES-005 is Draft planning for embedding provenance and reproducibility; its prohibited scope includes live embeddings and product implementation.
POSTGRES-006 is Draft runtime-boundary planning and prohibits live redb/.rvf mutation, model training, agent execution, and product implementation.
POSTGRES-009 defines a future end-to-end proof and prohibits live database mutation before the execution gate, profile installation, production deployment, and generated-runtime edits.
The proof record validates claim capture/mapping and reports queued_claim_count 13 and ready_count 0; it contains no vectorization benchmark result.

Recorded commands validate packet hashes, mappings, normalization, and planning-spine gates. The summary says the atoms are unresolved and queued; no benchmark command or latency/throughput receipt appears.
The current RuVector client says the REST surface is not visible, paths are unverified, and write operations remain out of scope.
The client is read-only and only fetches vector DB/GNN cache statistics through explicitly unverified best-guess paths; it does not vectorize data.
Current code serializes an already-existing &[f32] to bytes, validates dimensions, and upserts bytes to SQLite. This is encoding/persistence, not embedding generation or measured vectorization.

Tests prove bit-for-bit encode/decode and SQLite round-trip correctness for small hand-written vectors; they collect no latency percentiles, throughput, model provenance, workload version, hardware receipt, quality, or cold/warm data.
The root package scripts expose development, build, tests, planning verification, Tauri, design, and check commands but no vectorization benchmark command.

Reference source exposes several materially different embedding providers; the default is a fast hash-based non-semantic placeholder, while ONNX/Candle/API providers are alternatives. NBSOURCE-032 pins none of them.

The provider interface includes dimensions, and the default HashEmbedding is explicitly not semantic and is intended only for testing/prototyping, demonstrating why speed without quality/model identity is insufficient.

The reference test pins a 128-dimensional hash placeholder only for output length, determinism, difference, normalization, and provider name; it is not a performance benchmark and is not mapped to YNP-CLAIM-007.

A local synthetic 384-dimensional search result contains QPS, latency percentiles, recall, and empty metadata, but does not identify an embedding model/vectorizer, hardware/configuration, warm/cold protocol, persistence boundary, or YNP-CLAIM-007 workload; it cannot prove in-memory vectorization speed.

missing_evidence: A precise, measurable definition of "instantly," including the latency bound and exact start/stop event.
A claim-scoped runnable benchmark mapping NBSOURCE-032/YNP-CLAIM-007 to the exact in-memory-input -> vectorizer -> vector output operation.
Pinned vectorizer implementation and version/commit, model identity/version/weights hash, tokenizer/preprocessing version, output dimensions, batch sizes, and concurrency.
A versioned and content-hashed workload/corpus with schemas, sizes, distributions, expected outputs, and deterministic fixture generation.

A complete hardware and configuration receipt: CPU/GPU/NPU model, core/thread settings, RAM, storage, OS/kernel, Nix closure or dependency lock, compiler/build flags, runtime settings, and power/thermal state.
Documented cold-start and warmed-run protocols, repetition counts, cache-reset policy, outlier policy, and raw samples.
Vectorization-specific p50, p95, and p99 latency plus throughput; existing reference-only search/tokenizer numbers are different operations.

Quality/accuracy evidence appropriate to the pinned model and workload, with a ground truth or acceptance threshold; speed from the non-semantic hash placeholder would not establish useful vectorization.
An explicit persistence boundary and separate timing for in-memory generation, byte encoding, database/.rvf/redb/PostgreSQL write, flush/fsync/commit, and read-back verification.

A current installed-runtime receipt showing that the active profile frontdoor exposes the same pinned vectorizer/model and dimensions, plus concrete generated-runtime identity and benchmark logs. The yzx owner/frontdoor was resolved, but no relevant configured/generated surface was found and no installed product runtime was executed.
Materialized POSTGRES-005, POSTGRES-006, and POSTGRES-009 plan/proof artifacts. Every path listed for those future artifacts was absent in the current checkout.

A lawful execution authorization that permits building/running a benchmark and producing benchmark artifacts. This pass was read-only, and meta-ruvector build/test/product/runtime execution was explicitly forbidden.

claims.conclusion: Qualified by a narrower local capability, not by the claimed native Nushell/Yazelix path. The handoff peer deterministically maps ledger events to 384-dimensional hash-based pseudo-embeddings and best-effort writes them to a gitignored .handoff/ledger.db.rvf sidecar; a concrete local artifact exists. The active profile-owned CodeDB plugin has no RVF writer or vectorizer, and its capture backends are redb or PostgreSQL. meta-ruvector was inspected only as a read-only format/writer reference and was never built, tested, executed, or modified.

The only recorded evidence is NotebookLM source/source-relation evidence; it explicitly verifies no implementation and repetition closes no claim.
PGAUTH-003 is Draft and requires per-agent identity, scope, portability, retention, provenance, promotion, recovery, and proof that AgentDB does not own shared truth.

POSTGRES-005 and POSTGRES-006 are Draft planning tasks; live embeddings/RVF mutation are blocked, and the acceptance contract still requires reproducible embeddings, authority separation, ordered restartable recovery, and portable identities.
STORE-001 remains Draft and requires a David-approved, exactly-once data-class authority decision across redb, PostgreSQL plus RuVector, and CodeDB.
The current capture backend branches only between PgStore and the redb CaptureBatcher; no RVF backend or vectorizer is present.
Capture defaults to .codedb/store.redb; pg-prefixed specs select PostgreSQL; the store is opened once and batches are durably committed.
The backend contract names redb and PostgreSQL and defines SHA-256 content-addressed blob persistence, not vector or RVF persistence.
The PostgreSQL store explicitly creates the live-table shape minus the optional embedding column and persists module path, content, SHA-256, and metadata.
Handoff documents a separate RVF semantic-recall sidecar over an authoritative structured ledger and uses deterministic 384-dimensional hash-based pseudo-embeddings.
encode_event_to_vector deterministically derives a 384-dimensional vector; rvf_path maps the ledger path to a db.rvf sidecar.

append writes the authoritative event first, computes the embedding, then calls RvfStore::ingest_batch; the ingest Result is discarded, so RVF failure does not fail the append and cross-store atomicity is not provided.
Source tests assert deterministic 384-dimensional encoding and semantic recall, but they do not pin or hash a deterministic RVF file fixture or inject a torn/crash write.
RVF runtime/index/types/crypto are local path dependencies into ../../meta-ruvector, not commit- or content-pinned dependencies.
The default v2 feature adds RVF semantic recall over the redb authoritative store; legacy SQLite is import-only.
RVF is classified as a gitignored, regenerable cache rather than durable truth.
Committed continuity truth is deterministic JSONL; the RVF sidecar remains a gitignored local rebuild cache.
rebuild_from_jsonl opens crate::v1::Ledger and re-appends into the authoritative redb ledger; this inspected path does not recreate or verify the RVF sidecar.
REFERENCE-ONLY: current local package metadata identifies rvf-runtime 0.3.0 and rvf-types/index/quant path versions, but handoff consumes this checkout by path rather than a pinned source identity.
REFERENCE-ONLY: RvfStore::create creates a new file, acquires a writer lock, generates file identity, and writes an initial manifest.
REFERENCE-ONLY: ingest_batch validates vector dimensions and appends a VEC segment.
REFERENCE-ONLY: vector bytes are fsynced, epoch advances, optional witness is appended, and a manifest is written.
REFERENCE-ONLY: write_manifest appends identity-bearing manifest metadata and fsyncs the file.
REFERENCE-ONLY: the documented append protocol is segment fsync followed by manifest fsync.
REFERENCE-ONLY: VEC payload encodes dimension, count, vector ID, and little-endian f32 bytes.
REFERENCE-ONLY: segment headers are 64 bytes and the current segment format version is 1.
REFERENCE-ONLY: new file identity is derived from path plus current timestamp, so byte-identical deterministic file output is not established by deterministic vectors alone.

Active frontdoor resolves to /nix/store/rd29hc9xjbsv9p2i86q90k038j5glxvf-lifeos-foundation-yzx/bin/yzx. The package contains codedb and nu_plugin_codedb, and the plugin help reports MessagePack encoding, but closure/config/generated searches found no RVF/AgentDB/RuVector/ruvllm artifact or command.

Installed binary SHA-256 is 149537bbba39583451d0c33c0313d7a40b618b41b5fa4fcdaefd3b7a613c5127; direct --help exited 0 and listed native CodeDB commands with MessagePack encoding but no vectorize/RVF command; an RVF/AgentDB/RuVector string probe had no match.

A separate handoff artifact exists: size 145038 bytes, SHA-256 16f97ab544662858b1f91456146c39ea9ee5ac12ba43b95e0005df4c4d2b1393, header begins SFVR/version 1, and git check-ignore classifies it under .handoff/**/*.rvf. It is not a deterministic NBSOURCE-032 fixture.

missing_evidence: A pinned RVF format and writer source actually consumed by the current Yazelix/Nushell implementation. Handoff uses mutable local path dependencies into reference-only meta-ruvector; nu_plugin/yazelix have no RVF dependency or writer call path.
A claim-scoped producer-to-consumer trace from native Nushell structured values through a named vectorizer/model/dimension into an RVF writer, including error propagation and ordering.

A deterministic RVF fixture produced by that call path with stable fixture inputs, byte/file identity, reproducible SHA-256, and a regeneration receipt. Handoff's deterministic vector test does not hash a file, and RVF file_id generation includes path plus current timestamp.

An approved write-authority matrix for vector identities and data classes, including per-agent scope, PostgreSQL/shared-state boundary, retention, promotion, secret policy, and AgentDB relationship. PGAUTH-003, POSTGRES-005, POSTGRES-006, and STORE-001 remain Draft.

Atomicity proof across source persistence, vectorization, RVF VEC segment, manifest commit, and failure handling. The only local non-reference writer discards ingest_batch errors after authoritative append.

A fault-injection crash/torn-write test covering interruption before/after vector fsync and manifest fsync, followed by open/recovery and identity/hash verification. Parser truncation tests and stale-lock tests are not this proof.

Retention and recovery proof that reconstructs and verifies the RVF sidecar. The inspected handoff JSONL recovery implementation rebuilds crate::v1 redb state and does not re-embed or hash-verify RVF output.

Installed-runtime proof under the active profile showing the claimed RVF writer is present and materializes a claim-scoped RVF output. The frontdoor and MessagePack plugin are proven, but the RVF behavior is not.

A source/runtime version correlation proving the installed nu_plugin_codedb binary was built from the inspected commit and an RVF-capable dependency graph; current installed closure and binary/help probes show no RVF surface.

claims.conclusion: A narrower current-source statement is proven: envctl commit fd3d423fff767b4b96f67fdc46a8aac93da21559 contains a default-OFF `ruvector` semantic adapter that reads `embedding_minilm` vectors keyed by `module_path`, constructs pinned `ruvector-core` 2.2.3 `VectorDB`, and calls `insert`; that pinned package enables its redb-backed `storage` feature by default and writes bincode-encoded `Vec<f32>` values to redb table `vectors` under the vector ID in one committed write transaction. The broad source claim is not fully verified as deployed LifeOS behavior: the envctl feature is off by default, no installed envctl frontdoor was found, the active profile `codedb` runtime exposes source-blob tables rather than a vector table, no current dynamic transaction/crash trace was permitted under the hard read-only/no-generated-files constraint, and the required synchronization/authority decision remains Draft with its expected decision/proof artifacts absent. Thus 'vectors can be written to redb' is source-backed for a pinned optional adapter, but redb authority, installed activation, crash/replay guarantees, and whole-system synchronization are unresolved.

evidence.detail: Pins the NotebookLM object and SHA-256 and explicitly says source wording proves no implementation, authority, readiness, performance, or citation claim.
Separates instant vectorization, .rvf writing, redb writing, and no-JSON comparison into distinct claims; YNP-CLAIM-009 is only the redb assertion.
Marks YNP-CLAIM-009 source-only and unverified; schema, key/value encoding, transaction boundary, writer, durability, synchronization direction, recovery, and authority are absent from the source.
The claim remains queued and requires pinned adapter source, schema/key identity, transaction tests, crash/replay, synchronization map, and owner decision.
Defines the envctl semantic consumer over exported PostgreSQL/CodeDB `embedding_minilm` vectors and states that it is entirely behind the default-OFF `ruvector` feature.
The application row identity is `module_path`; the vector field is `Option<Vec<f32>>` and is documented as MiniLM 384-dimensional in production.
`SemanticIndex::from_rows` constructs `VectorDB::with_dimensions`, sets VectorEntry ID to module_path, places module_path in metadata, and calls `db.insert` with the vector.
ruvector-core is optional; engine default features are empty; the `ruvector` feature must be enabled explicitly.
Pins registry `ruvector-core = 2.2.3` with default features plus real-embeddings/uuid-support and explicitly forbids using the local meta-ruvector checkout.
Locks ruvector-core 2.2.3 at checksum 7f48f4d9950fc19c00d9a8854b6c5fdf0c974512646f13db98b19b4820d69d8d and its redb 2.6.3 dependency.
The pinned package's default features include `storage`, and `storage` enables redb and memmap2.
`with_dimensions` uses default DbOptions, and `VectorDB::insert` delegates the VectorEntry to storage before adding it to the search index.
Defines redb tables `vectors: &str -> &[u8]`, `metadata: &str -> &str`, and `config: &str -> &str`.
Validates dimensions, derives ID, begins a redb write transaction, bincode-encodes the vector, inserts it under the ID, writes optional JSON metadata under the same ID, and commits.

Defines a storage-feature test that inserts three vectors, drops the DB, reopens it, and checks vector count/get/search after restart; this is clean-restart evidence in source, not a current crash-injection receipt.
Current CodeDB redb schema is a source-blob store with schema, metadata, validation, source_blobs, source_files, and source_file_metadata tables; it has no vector table.
The CodeDB adapter writes raw bytes content-addressed by SHA-256 plus relative-path identity in one committed redb transaction; this proves a separate durable redb role, not vector semantics.
CodeDB owns file-to-datatable/code facts; envctl is downstream and must consume exports/checksums rather than redb internals.
Every runtime integration row forbids envctl redb access; source blobs are restored by SHA-256 before envctl consumes materialization rows.
PGAUTH-002 remains Draft and requires owner, lifetime, durability, identity, synchronization, crash-replay, recovery, observability, and explicit PostgreSQL/CodeDB relationships for every redb class.
STORE-001 is Draft; its gate requires a David-approved assignment of every data class and explicitly blocks live store mutation and source cutover.
POSTGRES-006 is Draft and still requires ordered, idempotent, restartable, observable flush/recovery without competing macro authority.

Active profile resolution was proven through `/home/flexnetos/.nix-profile/bin/yzx`; installed codedb 0.1.0 returned schema/tables successfully but exposed source_files/source_blobs and no vector table. No installed envctl command was found, so installed vector-write behavior is not proven.

claims.conclusion: The narrow protocol statement is verified, but the source's unbounded wording is not. Current source pins the Nushell plugin boundary to MsgPackSerializer, and an installed-runtime strace captured the encoder preamble `\7msgpack` plus binary MessagePack Call/CallResponse frames; a live negative comparison also showed that plugin `--format json` and `--format csv` both return the same native Nushell table shape, so those flags do not turn the plugin protocol payload into JSON text. However, there is no single current plugin-command-to-store path: plugin scan/export commands return native Values and ignore their signature-only `--store` option, while durable persistence is a separate `codedb capture` CLI path. That CLI passes `(String, Vec<u8>)` batches directly into BlobStore; redb inserts byte slices directly, but PostgreSQL separately constructs JSON text for its metadata jsonb column. In addition, plugin envctl-db compatibility commands explicitly request `--json` and parse JSON, import commands consume JSON artifacts, and CLI exports optionally serialize rows to JSON. Therefore 'no JSON text' is supportable only for the Nushell protocol payload and raw blob-content persistence, not for the whole command/store/export/compatibility surface. Required serializer-level instrumentation and a current pinned machine call graph remain absent, so verified would overstate the evidence.

evidence.detail: Canonical row records the claim but labels its absolute wording unverified and specifically requires an end-to-end trace, compatibility inventory, wire capture, and negative fixture.
YNP-CLAIM-010 remains queued for a pinned command-to-store graph, wire capture, serializer instrumentation, compatibility/export inventory, and distinguishing tests.

The extracted source sequence places MessagePack protocol, structured data, memory, vectorization, .rvf/redb writes, and the JSON comparison next to each other, making 'this path' broader and ambiguous rather than a precisely named boundary.
All atoms are queued; repetition verifies no claim, and installed-runtime ownership must be proved through the profile frontdoor.
The plugin imports MsgPackSerializer and also imports serde_json::Value, proving those are distinct surfaces in the same binary.
The executable calls serve_plugin(&CodeDbPlugin, MsgPackSerializer).

Profile binary resolves to /nix/store/9cymn7a4kj55ckjmxsn3yx2krqwzcw0w-codedb-runtime-tools-0.1.0/bin/nu_plugin_codedb. Live strace of `codedb tables` captured `write(1, "\7msgpack", 8)`, MessagePack Hello/Call/CallResponse frames, a binary Run request naming `codedb tables`, and a binary PipelineData response; command exited 0.

With isolated nonexistent HOME/XDG roots, installed Nu 0.113.1 loaded the installed plugin; `codedb export filesystem_entries ... --format json` and `--format csv` both described the same typed Nushell table, and the first row exposed typed native columns. JSON appeared only in the final explicit shell `to json --raw` display.

Plugin scan only scans and returns Value rows; plugin export says output remains native Nushell values and its run path returns rows_to_value. Neither run path opens or persists a store even though signatures expose --store.
The standalone CLI separates read-only scan from capture; only `codedb capture` invokes capture_rows and then formats result rows.
capture_rows scans, reads source files as Vec<u8>, accumulates `(relative_path, bytes)` batches, and calls BlobStore::persist_batch without a JSON conversion in this path.
BlobStore persists `(String, Vec<u8>)` batches and specifies byte-exact read-back with no text normalization.
redb hashes raw bytes and inserts `bytes.as_slice()` into the blob table, with path/ref strings in companion tables; no JSON serializer is called in this persistence function.
PostgreSQL content is bound as raw bytea, but batch_metadata_json constructs JSON text and SQL casts `$4::text::jsonb`; this directly prevents extending 'no JSON text' to every store field.
envctl-db plugin commands explicitly shell out to envctl with --json and parse stdout with serde_json::from_slice.
Standalone CLI OutputFormat::Json explicitly calls serde_json::to_string_pretty; NUON and CSV are separate projection branches.

Existing unit tests intentionally require --json for envctl compatibility and test conversion from JSON into typed Nushell values; they do not instrument the MsgPack serializer or assert that protocol payloads never call serde_json.

The transient smoke explicitly runs the plugin and only then pipes native output through Nushell `to json`, demonstrating a JSON presentation surface after the plugin protocol rather than proving JSON-free internals.
Nix package owner builds codedb and nu_plugin_codedb together as codedb-runtime-tools 0.1.0.
The locked Cargo source is built and both release binaries are installed into the Nix output.
RECOVERY-002 is Draft and requires evidence-backed reconciliation of CodeDB capabilities and gaps.
ADOPT-001 is Draft and requires behavioral boundaries, known gaps, current paths, and parity gates for adopted storage capability.
POSTGRES-004 is Draft and requires lossless ingestion, identity, checksums, exactly-once behavior, failure recovery, and reconstruction tests.
POSTGRES-009 is Draft and requires a bounded source-to-database-to-projection-to-build-to-runtime round trip; that full path is not current proof for this claim.

missing_evidence: A serializer-instrumented run proving which serializer functions execute for the Run request and PipelineData response, not merely syscall-level binary frames.

A current, commit-pinned machine call graph for HEAD 9451b20. The available GitKB index is from 2026-07-02 on branch label main, has 6559 unresolved calls, lacks current capture/PgStore symbols, and reports stale line positions; GitNexus indexes only meta.

A repository test that fails if protocol payloads invoke serde_json while explicitly allowing envctl compatibility JSON, JSON/JSONC/Nix import artifacts, PostgreSQL metadata jsonb, CLI JSON projection, and shell `to json` display.

An implemented plugin command that actually reaches BlobStore::persist_batch. Current plugin commands expose signature-level --store flags but their run paths do not open or write a store; durable capture is a separate standalone CLI command.

A bounded end-to-end store fixture/wire trace covering both redb and PostgreSQL without live database mutation, including PostgreSQL protocol parameter types and confirmation that only metadata, not blob content, uses JSON.

Any current local primary implementation path from plugin native values through vectorization into .rvf or redb; the extracted source implies that sequence, but current CodeDB plugin source does not implement it.
Completed proof records for RECOVERY-002, ADOPT-001, POSTGRES-004, and POSTGRES-009; all four canonical tasks remain Draft and their proof files are absent.

claims.conclusion: Verified strictly as a question-claim. The locally retained exact fulltext asks, on line 7, whether to explore how Yazelix and Nushell initialize in the background once a portable release folder is launched; SHA-256, byte count, source ID, and notebook ID match the validated packet. This wording neither asserts that such initialization exists nor authorizes portable-release execution. The separate installed-runtime trace proves the current narrower profile-owned runtime: Yazelix source defines packages.x86_64-linux.lifeos_foundation_yzx, the Nix profile resolves /home/flexnetos/.nix-profile/bin/yzx to /nix/store/rd29...-lifeos-foundation-yzx/bin/yzx, relevant installed launch scripts are hash-identical to current source, generated Nushell config links the packaged config with /home/flexnetos/.config/yazelix/shell_nu.nu, and a live Mars -> Zellij -> yzx/yzx_control -> Nushell tree uses /home/flexnetos/.local/share/yazelix. That runtime evidence does not prove that the Nix store output is the source's undefined 'portable release folder,' and the full failure/shutdown/rollback lifecycle remains unproven.
